metadata.title: Developing Cellular and Gene Therapy Products: Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)

rules.rule_text: Commercial INDs for CGT products must be submitted via the Electronic Submission Gateway (ESG) in eCTD format per section 745A(a)(2) of the FD&C Act; noncommercial INDs are encouraged but not required to submit in eCTD format.
rules.verbatim_quote: Commercial INDs must be submitted via ESG in eCTD format (section 745A(a)(2) of FD&C Act); noncommercial INDs encouraged but not required to submit in eCTD.
rules.exceptions: Noncommercial INDs are encouraged but not required to use eCTD format.

rules.rule_text: An IND must include Form FDA 1571 signed by the sponsor or authorized representative; for foreign sponsors, an additional signature from a U.S. attorney, agent, or official is required.
rules.verbatim_quote: IND must include Form FDA 1571 (signed by sponsor or authorized representative); foreign sponsors must include additional signature from U.S. attorney/agent/official.

rules.rule_text: IND amendments for manufacturing changes that include numerous or significant changes should include a Reviewer's Guide and allow at least 30 days lead time before FDA review is completed so that new clinical trial material is not released prematurely.
rules.verbatim_quote: IND amendments for manufacturing changes with numerous/significant changes should include Reviewer's Guide and allow ≥30 days lead time for FDA review before releasing new clinical trial material.
rules.exceptions: Applies to amendments with numerous or significant manufacturing changes.

rules.rule_text: Form FDA 3674 (clinical trial registration certification) must be submitted with the IND and must contain the NCT control numbers for any applicable clinical trials.
rules.verbatim_quote: Form FDA 3674 (clinical trial registration certification) must be submitted and contain NCT control numbers.

rules.rule_text: An IND goes into effect 30 calendar days after FDA receives it unless FDA notifies the sponsor of a clinical hold or grants earlier approval per 21 CFR 312.40(b).
rules.verbatim_quote: An IND goes into effect 30 calendar days after FDA receives it, unless FDA notifies sponsor of clinical hold or earlier approval (21 CFR 312.40(b)).
rules.exceptions: FDA may grant earlier approval or place product on clinical hold before 30-day period expires.

rules.rule_text: Manufacturing changes that constitute 'essential information' must be submitted as IND amendments per 21 CFR 312.31(a)(1) and must be submitted before the changed product is used in clinical investigations.
rules.verbatim_quote: Manufacturing changes considered 'essential information' must be submitted as IND amendment per 21 CFR 312.31(a)(1); must be submitted before use of changed product in clinical investigations.

rules.rule_text: New or revised clinical protocols may be implemented after submission to FDA and approval by the IRB per 21 CFR 312.30(a)-(b).
rules.verbatim_quote: New/revised clinical protocols may be implemented after submitted to FDA and approved by IRB per 21 CFR 312.30(a)-(b).

rules.rule_text: Rolling BLA review requires Fast Track, Breakthrough Therapy, or RMAT designation and FDA agreement to rolling review; no more than 12 months should elapse from first submission of BLA content to final submission completing the BLA; FDA's review clock starts when the final module is received.
rules.verbatim_quote: A drug may receive rolling review if it has Fast Track, Breakthrough Therapy, or Regenerative Medicine Advanced Therapy (RMAT) designation and if certain criteria are met; however, FDA must still agree to rolling review... no more than 12 months should elapse from the first submission of BLA content to the final submission to complete the BLA. FDA's review clock starts on the date the final module is received.
rules.exceptions: FDA will generally accept only complete modules for Modules 3, 4, and 5 under rolling review.

rules.rule_text: For autologous CGT products, donor eligibility determination under 21 CFR §1271.50 and donor screening/testing under 21 CFR §§1271.75, 1271.80, and 1271.85 are not required; however, the product must be labeled prominently with 'FOR AUTOLOGOUS USE ONLY' and, unless full screening is performed, 'NOT EVALUATED FOR INFECTIOUS SUBSTANCES'.
rules.verbatim_quote: A donor eligibility determination under 21 CFR § 1271.50 and donor screening or testing under 21 CFR §§ 1271.75, 1271.80, and 1271.85 are not required for cells and tissues used in the manufacture of autologous products... for products intended for autologous use, the manufacturer must prominently label the product with the statement, 'FOR AUTOLOGOUS USE ONLY.'

rules.rule_text: For autologous CGT products, FDA recommends including a minimum of two unique identifiers (e.g., donor identification number (DIN), product tracking number) to minimize the potential for patient mix-ups.
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA recommends that the manufacturer include a minimum of two unique identifiers (e.g., donor identification number (DIN), product tracking number, etc.) for autologous therapies to minimize potential for mix-ups.

rules.rule_text: For allogeneic CGT products, a responsible person must determine and document the eligibility of each cell or tissue donor per 21 CFR §1271.50(a); donor eligibility must be based on donor screening and testing per 21 CFR §§1271.75, 1271.80, and 1271.85; testing must be performed using FDA-licensed, approved, or cleared test kits per manufacturer's instructions in a CLIA-certified laboratory.
rules.verbatim_quote: For allogeneic donor material, manufacturers are required to determine whether a donor is eligible based upon the results of donor screening and testing in accordance with 21 CFR §§ 1271.75, 1271.80, and 1271.85. A responsible person must determine and document the eligibility of a cell or tissue donor. Testing must be performed using FDA-licensed, approved, or cleared test kits according to the manufacturer's instructions for use and must be performed in a Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments-certified laboratory.

rules.rule_text: Prior to initiating Phase 2 or 3 clinical investigations: (1) release tests must be qualified, must have predefined acceptance criteria (AC), and must comply with CGMP; (2) characterization tests do not need to be qualified, have AC, or comply with CGMP for testing and release; (3) release tests must be validated prior to BLA submission.
rules.verbatim_quote: Prior to initiating Phase 2 or 3 clinical investigations on the drug, release tests must be qualified, tests must have predefined AC, and tests must comply with current good manufacturing practice (CGMP). In contrast, characterization tests do not need to be qualified, have AC, or comply with the CGMP requirements for testing and release for distribution. Release tests must be validated prior to BLA submission.
rules.exceptions: Characterization tests are exempt from Phase 2/3 qualification requirements.

rules.rule_text: Release test results must be reported on the product certificate of analysis (COA); characterization test results are not reported on a COA; both types of results must be recorded and submitted to the IND or BLA in Module 3 of the CTD.
rules.verbatim_quote: Release test results should be reported on the product certificate of analysis (COA), whereas characterization test results are not reported on a COA. Both release and characterization test results should be recorded and submitted to an IND application or BLA at relevant places in Module 3 of the CTD.

rules.rule_text: Tests for mycoplasma and adventitious agents must be performed on cell culture harvest material prior to further processing; tests for sterility, endotoxin, and identity must be performed on formulated product in the final labeled container.
rules.verbatim_quote: Tests for mycoplasma and adventitious agents should be performed on cell culture harvest material prior to further processing. Tests for sterility, endotoxin, and identity should be performed on formulated product in the final labeled container to ensure that microbial contamination and product mix-ups (such as those that may occur during final DP manufacturing steps) do not occur.

rules.rule_text: Critical quality attributes (CQAs) must be established as early as possible in product development; well-established CQAs are generally necessary for assessing analytical comparability following manufacturing changes.
rules.verbatim_quote: It can be crucial to establish CQAs for a product as early as possible, particularly when sponsors plan to make manufacturing changes during product development, because well-established CQAs are generally necessary for assessing analytical comparability between different versions of a product.

rules.rule_text: For FIH studies, the IND must describe each non-compendial analytical method used to assess product quality with an evaluation of assay performance characteristics (accuracy, reproducibility, sensitivity, and specificity) to justify fitness for purpose; for GT products, the assay(s) used to determine dose (e.g., vector genome titer by qPCR, transducing units, plaque forming units) must be qualified prior to initiating clinical studies.
rules.verbatim_quote: For FIH studies, the IND should contain a description of each non-compendial analytical method used to assess quality of the product (DP, DS, and components), with an evaluation of assay performance characteristics (i.e., accuracy, reproducibility, sensitivity, and specificity) to justify that the method is fit for purpose... to assure safety of gene therapy (GT) products, the sponsor should qualify the assay(s) used to determine dose (e.g., vector genome titer by quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR), transducing units, plaque forming units, transduced cells) prior to initiating clinical studies.
rules.exceptions: Compendial methods (USP/NF) with established performance characteristics do not require additional fitness-for-purpose evaluation.

rules.rule_text: Final acceptance criteria (AC) for the drug substance (DS) and drug product (DP) are not expected until the end of clinical development per 21 CFR 312.23(a)(7)(i); an incremental approach to setting AC is acceptable throughout development.
rules.verbatim_quote: Final AC for the DS and DP are not expected until the end of clinical development.

rules.rule_text: Validation of a commercial manufacturing process must be supported by data from commercial-scale batches; scaled-down models are not appropriate for process performance qualification (PPQ), but may be used at process design and characterization stages; the scaled-down version must represent the intended commercial manufacturing process as closely as possible.
rules.verbatim_quote: The validation of a commercial manufacturing process should be supported by data from commercial-scale batches. Generally, the use of scaled-down models is not appropriate for process performance qualification (PPQ). However, scaled-down models can be used at the process design and characterization stages to evaluate process variability and to determine appropriate process parameters. Sponsors should demonstrate the validity of the scaled-down process, and the scaled-down version should represent the intended commercial manufacturing process as closely as possible.
rules.exceptions: Scaled-down models are acceptable for process design and characterization only, not PPQ.

rules.rule_text: There is no fixed number of PPQ lots recommended for process validation; the number must be informed by a risk assessment and must be sufficient to demonstrate that consecutive manufacturing runs consistently yield a product meeting acceptance criteria.
rules.verbatim_quote: There is no fixed number of lots recommended for PPQ. In general, a greater understanding and knowledge of the product and manufacturing process can reduce the number of PPQ lots that should be sufficient to qualify the performance of the manufacturing process. The number of PPQ lots should be informed by a risk assessment and should be sufficient to demonstrate that consecutive runs of the manufacturing process perform as expected and consistently yield a product that meets AC.

rules.rule_text: For FIH stability support, the sponsor must show that the product is within acceptable quality limits for the duration of the planned clinical study; stability data from nonclinical lots, engineering lots, or highly similar product lots stored in the same manner (same formulation, concentration, storage temperature, container) may be used to support Phase 1.
rules.verbatim_quote: Demonstrating product stability is needed at all stages of product development. Sponsors should be able to show that the product is within acceptable quality limits for the duration of the planned clinical study; however, an incremental approach may be appropriate for setting AC to support stability. For example, data to support stability of the product for Phase 1 studies can be based on data from nonclinical lots, engineering lots, or highly similar product lots that have been stored in the same manner as the clinical material (e.g., the same formulation, concentration, storage temperature, and container).

rules.rule_text: For BLA submission: the manufacturing process and all analytical methods must be validated per 21 CFR 211.165(e) and comply with 21 CFR 610; the BLA must contain data demonstrating safety, purity, and potency, a full description of manufacturing methods, and data establishing stability through the data period per 21 CFR 601.2(a); DP manufacturing must comply with 21 CFR Part 211, except that most Phase 1 investigational drugs are exempt from Part 211 requirements per 21 CFR 210.2(c).
rules.verbatim_quote: For a BLA submission, the manufacturing process and all analytical methods performed to support product quality must be validated and comply with the regulations in 21 CFR 610... A BLA must contain, among other information, data which demonstrate that the product meets requirements of safety, purity and potency, a full description of manufacturing methods and data establishing stability of the product through the data period. Process characterization and validation studies that must be conducted to meet CGMP are outlined in 21 CFR 211 Subpart F — Production and Process Controls.
rules.exceptions: Most Phase 1 investigational drugs are exempt from 21 CFR Part 211 requirements per 21 CFR 210.2(c).

rules.rule_text: When selecting an animal species for nonclinical pharmacology/toxicology (PT) studies, key considerations include: (1) whether the CGT product is pharmacologically active in the species; (2) feasibility of using the intended clinical delivery device or procedure; (3) comparability of physiology and anatomy for the route of administration and target sites; (4) sensitivity of the species to potential toxicities; for GT products, additional considerations include vector permissiveness, transduction profile, and pharmacological response to the vector and transgene.
rules.verbatim_quote: When selecting an animal species for nonclinical PT studies, key considerations include whether the investigational CGT product is pharmacologically active in the species, the technical feasibility of using the intended clinical delivery device or procedure for product administration, comparability of the physiology and anatomy between animals and humans for the ROA and target anatomic sites that the product is intended to reach, and the sensitivity of the selected species to potential toxicities for the product. Additional considerations for GT products include the permissiveness or susceptibility of the species to the vector, vector transduction profile, and the pharmacological response to the vector and the expressed transgene.
rules.exceptions: Non-animal test methods may be acceptable if proposed in early communication meetings with scientific justification.

rules.rule_text: A comprehensive discussion, with supporting data, regarding the biological relevance of each animal model to the proposed patient population must be provided in pre-IND and IND submissions; this discussion must address at minimum: (1) disease phenotype/injury progression in the model; (2) model lifespan; (3) similarities and differences between model and patient population; (4) timing of product administration relative to disease onset; and (5) relevant anatomy and physiology for the delivery method and target site(s).
rules.verbatim_quote: The sponsor should provide scientific justification in pre-IND and IND submissions for the animal model/species selection. A comprehensive discussion, with supporting data, regarding the biological relevancy of each animal model should be provided. This should include, but is not limited to, the following: (1) Progression of the disease phenotype or injury observed in each animal model; (2) The lifespan of each model; (3) The similarities and differences between the animal model(s) and the proposed patient population; (4) The timing of product administration relative to disease onset and progression as it pertains to the proposed patient population; and (5) A description of the relevant anatomy and physiology related to the delivery method and target anatomic site(s) in animals versus humans.

rules.rule_text: When animal models of the target disease are not available or the CGT product is incompatible with an animal model, the sponsor must provide supporting data from alternative sources (e.g., in vitro studies, in silico studies, in vivo studies using an analogous animal product, or nonclinical/clinical data from related products); if no disease model exists for activity and safety evaluation, pivotal safety studies must be conducted in healthy animals.
rules.verbatim_quote: When animal models of the target disease are not available or if the investigational CGT product is incompatible with an animal model, the sponsor should provide supporting data from other sources. Some examples include in vitro studies, in silico studies, in vivo studies using an analogous animal product, and relevant nonclinical or clinical data from studies evaluating a related product or indication... If there are no available animal models of the target disease to evaluate activity and safety of the CGT product, the pivotal safety studies are typically conducted in healthy animals to identify potential toxicities related to the CGT product or administration procedure(s).

rules.rule_text: Proposals for alternative test methods or new approach methodologies (NAMs) in place of animal studies must be submitted during early communication meetings with FDA; FDA will consider alternatives backed by science and producing scientifically valid data and will evaluate whether the alternative could be used in place of an animal test method within a particular context of use.
rules.verbatim_quote: Proposals, with justification for any potential alternative approaches (e.g., in vitro or in silico testing), should be submitted during early communication meetings with FDA... FDA is open to alternative methods that are backed by science and produce scientifically valid data and will consider whether such an alternative could be used in place of an animal test method within a particular context of use.

rules.rule_text: Tumorigenicity studies are necessary for pluripotent stem cell-derived CGT products due to the potential for aberrant cell proliferation, differentiation, and teratoma formation; the animal species/strain for in vivo tumorigenicity assessment must be permissive to engraftment and long-term survival of the investigational product administered via the planned clinical route; if the sponsor considers tumorigenicity studies unnecessary, a scientific justification with supporting data must be submitted to OTP for review.
rules.verbatim_quote: Tumorigenicity studies are usually necessary for pluripotent stem cell-derived products, which have the potential for aberrant cell proliferation, differentiation, and teratoma formation... The animal species/strain for in vivo assessment of tumorigenicity should be permissive to the engraftment and long-term survival of the investigational product following administration via the planned clinical ROA... If the sponsor considers a tumorigenicity study unnecessary, they should provide a scientific justification with supporting data in their submission to OTP for review.
rules.exceptions: An analogous animal product is typically not appropriate for tumorigenicity testing.

rules.rule_text: Proof-of-concept (POC) studies for CGT products must evaluate bioactivity, determine feasibility of the route of administration, characterize the putative mechanism of action, and aid in determining an active dose level range and optimized dosing regimen for the initial clinical trial; pharmacology summaries and final study reports for each POC study must be included in the IND submission.
rules.verbatim_quote: POC studies are important to evaluate bioactivity, determine the feasibility of the ROA, and provide a rationale for use of an investigational CGT product in the target clinical population. POC studies often characterize the putative mechanism of action of the investigational CGT product and aid in determining a potentially active dose level range and optimized dosing regimen for the initial clinical trial... The sponsor should provide pharmacology summaries and final study reports for each POC study in the IND submission.

rules.rule_text: An IND must contain adequate information about pharmacological and toxicological studies of the CGT product based on which the sponsor concludes it is reasonably safe to conduct the proposed clinical investigations per 21 CFR 312.23(a)(8); if a sponsor believes adequate toxicological information can be provided without further toxicology testing, a scientific rationale for this conclusion must be included in the IND.
rules.verbatim_quote: An IND must contain adequate information about pharmacological and toxicological studies of the drug involving laboratory animals or in vitro, on the basis of which the sponsor has concluded that it is reasonably safe to conduct the proposed clinical investigations. If a sponsor believes adequate information about toxicological studies of the drug can be provided without further toxicology testing for a specific product, they should provide a discussion of the available data to support the safety profile of the investigational product and their scientific rationale for why they believe further toxicological assessment is unnecessary in their IND submission.

rules.rule_text: For single-dose administration CGT products, the pivotal toxicology/safety study must be of sufficient duration to evaluate potential acute and long-term toxicities and the potential for resolution or stabilization; multiple sacrifice time points must be included; the sponsor must provide scientific rationale justifying the dose levels, sacrifice timepoints, and duration of the safety/toxicology studies based on the biodistribution and persistence profile.
rules.verbatim_quote: The duration for a pivotal toxicology/safety study evaluating a single-dose administration will vary based on the product characteristics and ROA for the intended clinical population. The study duration should be informed by the biodistribution (BD) and persistence profile of the investigational CGT product... The pivotal toxicology/safety study should be of sufficient duration to evaluate potential acute and long-term toxicities, as well as the potential for resolution or stabilization of any findings. Multiple sacrifice time points following administration of an investigational product should be included to comprehensively characterize potential adverse findings.

rules.rule_text: For GT biodistribution studies, quantitative and sensitive assays such as qPCR are recommended for analyzing vector distribution and persistence in various tissues and biofluids; for PCR-positive samples, transgene mRNA and/or protein expression levels must also be measured using an appropriate method.
rules.source_section: V.F Design of Cell Distribution/Biodistribution Studies
rules.verbatim_quote: For GT products, use of a quantitative and sensitive assay such as qPCR is recommended to analyze vector BD and persistence in various tissues/biofluids. For samples that are determined to be positive for vector presence upon PCR analysis, transgene mRNA and/or protein expression levels should also be measured using an appropriate method.
rules.exceptions: CT products may use imaging modalities, genetically modified cells, nanoparticle-labeled cells, or qPCR/immunohistochemistry.

rules.rule_text: For CGT clinical trials, an adequate and well-controlled study requires: (1) a valid comparison with a control group; (2) a suitable method of assignment to treatment and control groups (e.g., randomization); and (3) adequate measures to minimize bias (e.g., blinding of study subjects and/or evaluators); for rare diseases with a stable natural history, a well-conducted natural history study may serve as an acceptable external or historical control.
rules.verbatim_quote: Some of the features of an adequate and well-controlled clinical study include a valid comparison with a control to provide a quantitative assessment of drug effect, a suitable method of assignment to treatment and control groups (e.g., randomization), and adequate measures to minimize bias (e.g., blinding of study subjects and/or evaluators).
rules.exceptions: For rare diseases with stable natural history that does not improve with other interventions, a natural history study may serve as acceptable external/historical control.

rules.rule_text: Substantial evidence of effectiveness generally requires two adequate and well-controlled clinical investigations, each convincing on its own; FDA may conclude that one adequate and well-controlled clinical study plus confirmatory evidence constitutes substantial evidence when specific factors support reliance on a single trial (e.g., disease seriousness, ethical/practical constraints on conducting multiple trials, persuasiveness and robustness of the single study).
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA has interpreted the substantial clinical evidence requirement as generally requiring two adequate and well-controlled clinical investigations, each convincing on its own, to establish effectiveness. FDA may, however, conclude that one adequate and well-controlled clinical study plus confirmatory evidence is sufficient to establish effectiveness.
rules.exceptions: For rare diseases where ethical or practical constraints prevent two adequate and well-controlled studies, one study plus confirmatory evidence may suffice.
